steps.title: Track the passage's logic
steps.content: Mensah finds a negative correlation: as natural-resource extraction grows as a share of a developing country's economy, foreign investment falls. The passage calls this "counterintuitive" (since extraction needs large initial investment) and then explains the cause—boom-bust cycles, currency instability, and vulnerability to shocks create uncertainty that investors avoid.

steps.title: Define what the main idea must contain
steps.content: The right answer must include (1) the surprise/expectation reversal and (2) the actual cause—instability/uncertainty driving investors away. Anything that misnames the cause or reverses the direction fails.

steps.title: Eliminate by misread/overreach
steps.content:
• Choice A — ‘Although it may seem surprising that foreign investment declines in developing countries as natural-resource extraction makes up a larger share of those countries' economies, that decline happens because resource extraction requires initial investments too large for foreign investors to supply.’: misnames the cause—the passage says scale is exactly what multinationals CAN meet; the actual cause is instability, not investor inability. ✗
  • Choice B — ‘Although developing countries tend to become less dependent on foreign investment as natural-resource industries make up a larger share of their economies, this change may not occur if the boom-bust cycle of those industries destabilizes local currencies or increases countries' vulnerability to external shocks.’: misframes the finding as countries choosing independence; the passage describes investors choosing to withdraw due to risk. ✗
  • Choice C — ‘Although one might expect that foreign investment would increase as natural-resource extraction makes up a larger share of developing countries' economies, the opposite happens because heavy reliance on natural resources can lead to unattractive conditions for investors.’: captures both the expectation reversal and the correct cause (unattractive/unstable conditions). ✓
  • Choice D — ‘Although foreign investors tend to avoid initial investments in natural-resource industries in developing countries, foreign investment may increase significantly as those industries stabilize and the risks associated with them decline.’: reverses the direction—the passage explains why investment falls, not how it might rise. ✗
That confirms C is correct.
